content: ADPRO SMTP setup To receive event for ADPRO dveice type, following steps can be followed. login to AdproXoClient. Add device. Go to System / Connections / E-mail and configure SMTP details Go to Behaviour / E-mail Addressbook and add destination email and Triggers Make sure for each required alarm profile the Email transmition checkbox is enabled.

title: Axis FTP
excerpt: AXIS FTP ConfigurationUsing FTP Recipient & RulesGCX-Surge Analytics Integration GuideOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on an AXIS device. The device will send snapshots to the GCX-Surge FTP ingest endpoint wh
content: AXIS FTP ConfigurationUsing FTP Recipient & RulesGCX-Surge Analytics Integration GuideOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on an AXIS device. The device will send snapshots to the GCX-Surge FTP ingest endpoint whenever a configured event condition is triggered, enabling the Analytics service to process incoming event data.Configuration involves two parts:Adding an FTP Recipient (surge-ftp) with the GCX FTP server credentialsCreating a Rule (surge-ftp) that triggers the FTP upload action on a chosen event conditionPrerequisitesBefore starting, ensure the following are in place:FTP credentials from the GCX Device Add API response (host, port, username, password)The AXIS device must have active internet accessAdministrator credentials for the AXIS device web interfaceA valid GCX-Surge device registration (Device Add API call completed)Configuration StepsFollow the steps below in order. All settings are accessible from the System > Events section.1Open a browser and navigate to the AXIS device IP address. Sign in with administrator credentials.2Scroll to the bottom toolbar and click System, then click Events.3Click the Recipients tab. Click + to add a new recipient.4Set Type to FTP and fill in the Host, Port, Username, and Password from the GCX API response.5Click Test to verify the FTP connection. If successful, click Save.6Click the Rules tab. Click + to add a new rule named surge-ftp.7Set the Condition to your desired event detector (e.g. Perimeter Defender intrusion-1).8Set Action to Send images through FTP and select surge-ftp as the Recipient.9Configure stream profile, buffers, filename format, then click Save.10Verify by triggering an event — images will appear in the GCX platform.Step 1 — Login to the AXIS DeviceOpen a web browser and enter the device IP address (e.g. http://10.66.1.102). Sign in with administrator credentials. After login, scroll to the bottom toolbar and click System, then click Events.The Events page displays four tabs: Rules, Schedules, Recipients, and Manual triggers.Step 2 — Add an FTP RecipientA Recipient defines the FTP server where event images are uploaded. Configure this before creating rules.Click the Recipients tabClick the + (Add recipient) button at the bottom of the recipient listFill in the recipient details as shown in the table belowFieldValueNamesurge-ftp (or any descriptive label)TypeFTPHost51.102.164.94 (from GCX API ftpConfig)Port21FolderLeave blank (uploads to root directory)UsernameuniqueId value from GCX Device Add API responsePasswordPassword from GCX Device Add API responseUse temporary file namesUncheckedUse passive FTPUncheckedClick Test to verify the FTP connection — a success message confirms the credentials are correctClick Save to save the recipientThe Host and Username values are provided in the ftpConfig object returned by the GCX Device Add API (POST /api/v1/device). Use the uniqueId field as the FTP username. Fig 1 — AXIS Recipients Tab showing surge-ftp FTP recipient configuration with host, port, username and passwordStep 3 — Create an Event RuleA Rule defines when to upload images (the Condition) and where to send them (the Action). Follow these steps to create the surge-ftp rule:Click the Rules tabClick the + (Add rule) button to create a new ruleConfigure the rule fields as described belowRule SettingsFieldValueNamesurge-ftpUse this ruleChecked (enabled)Wait between actions00:00:00 (adjust as needed)Condition SettingsFieldValueConditionPerimeter Defender intrusion-1 (or your chosen detector)Invert this conditionUncheckedUse this condition as triggerCheckedAction SettingsFieldValueActionSend images through FTPRecipientsurge-ftpStream profileGcxCustom image frequencyOffPrebuffer (seconds)03Postbuffer (mm:ss)00:05Maximum images0 (unlimited)Create folderLeave blankFilenameimage/%y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S-%f_its.jpgMaximum sequence number0 (unlimited)Click Save to save the ruleEnsure Use this rule is checked and the green dot appears next to the rule name — this confirms the rule is active and will trigger on the next event.Fig 2 — AXIS Rules Tab showing surge-ftp rule with Perimeter Defender condition and Send images through FTP actionStep 4 — Verify Event UploadTrigger an event on the camera (e.g. walk through the detection zone). The device will capture images and upload them to the GCX FTP server. Verify by checking the GCX platform — the event should appear with the uploaded snapshots.Success: When the event is received and displayed in the GCX platform, the FTP configuration is complete and working correctly.

title: Dahua FTP
excerpt: FTP-Based Event TransmissionDahua Camera Configuration GuideIntegration with GCX-Surge Analytics ServiceOverviewThis document describes how to configure FTP-based event transmission on a Dahua IP camera so that snapshots and recordings are automatica
content: FTP-Based Event TransmissionDahua Camera Configuration GuideIntegration with GCX-Surge Analytics ServiceOverviewThis document describes how to configure FTP-based event transmission on a Dahua IP camera so that snapshots and recordings are automatically uploaded to the GCX-Surge analytics platform. The configuration involves two main areas within the Dahua web portal:Storage > Destination > FTP — enter the FTP server credentialsStorage > Destination > Path — enable FTP as the destination for records and snapshots Configuration StepsFollow the steps below in order. All settings are accessible from the Setting tab in the top navigation bar. 1Log in to the Dahua camera web portal and click Setting in the top navigation bar.2Navigate to Storage > Destination > Path tab.3Under both the Record and Snapshot sections, check the FTP checkboxes for Scheduled, Event, and Alarm. Click Save.4Navigate to Storage > Destination > FTP tab.5Select FTP from the dropdown. Enter the Server Address, Port, Username, and Password obtained from the GCX Device Add API response.6Leave Remote Directory blank (files will be saved to the root directory).7Click test to verify the credentials. If the test succeeds, click Save.8Check the Enable checkbox and click Save again to activate FTP uploads.9Events (motion, alarm, scheduled snapshots) will now be automatically uploaded to the FTP server. Step 1 — Log In and Open SettingsLog in to the Dahua camera portal using its IP address (e.g., http://192.168.1.x). Click Setting in the top navigation bar to access the configuration area. Fig 1 — Dahua camera Settings portal (Camera > Conditions shown as reference)Steps 2 & 3 — Configure Storage Destination PathNavigate to Storage > Destination. On the Path tab, enable FTP for both Record and Snapshot by checking the Scheduled, Event, and Alarm checkboxes in the FTP row. Click Save. Note: Ensure FTP is checked for all three event types (Scheduled, Event, Alarm) under both Record and Snapshot to capture all upload scenarios. Fig 2 — Storage > Destination > Path: FTP enabled for all Record and Snapshot event typesSteps 4 – 7 — Enter FTP Credentials and TestGo to the FTP tab (Storage > Destination > FTP). Select FTP from the type dropdown and fill in the following fields: Server Address — IP or hostname from the GCX API ftpConfig (e.g., 51.102.164.94)Port — 21 (default FTP port)Username — uniqueId value returned by the GCX Device Add APIPassword — as provided in the API responseRemote Directory — leave blank to save in the root directory Click test. A success message confirms the credentials are correct. Then click Save. Fig 3 — FTP tab with credentials entered, Enable checkbox unchecked (before enabling)Step 8 — Enable FTP UploadsAfter a successful test, check the Enable checkbox at the top of the FTP configuration panel and click Save. FTP upload is now active. Note: The Enable checkbox must be checked and saved separately after testing. If it is left unchecked, no files will be uploaded even though the credentials are saved. Fig 4 — FTP tab with Enable checked and credentials saved — FTP uploads are now active

title: Ganz FTP
excerpt: GANZAI CAMERA SYSTEMFTP-Based Event TransmissionAI Rules — Shares Configuration GuideGCX-Surge Analytics IntegrationOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on a GANZ AI camera system. Once configured, the devi
content: GANZAI CAMERA SYSTEMFTP-Based Event TransmissionAI Rules — Shares Configuration GuideGCX-Surge Analytics IntegrationOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on a GANZ AI camera system. Once configured, the device will automatically upload event snapshots to the GCX-Surge FTP ingest server whenever an AI-triggered event occurs.Configuration is done entirely within the GANZ web portal under:Configuration > AI Rules > Shares > Network > FTPPrerequisitesFTP credentials from the GCX Device Add API response (host, port, username, password)Administrator access to the GANZ camera portalThe GANZ device must have active internet accessA completed GCX-Surge device registration (Device Add API call)Configuration StepsFollow the steps below in order.1Open a browser and navigate to the GANZ device IP address. Log in with administrator credentials.2Click Configuration in the top navigation bar.3In the left sidebar expand AI-RULES and click Shares.4Ensure the Network tab is selected, then click the FTP sub-tab.5Click the Add button to open the FTP configuration form.6Enter a Name (customer label), Host, Port, Username, and Password from the GCX API response.7Configure the File Path Format using the token builder — select tokens and click Use.8Click the TEST button to send a test file. Verify the upload succeeds.9Click APPLY to save the configuration. Events will now be uploaded to FTP automatically.Step 1 — Log In to the GANZ PortalOpen a web browser and enter the GANZ device IP address. The login page displays the GANZ logo with User ID and Password fields. Enter your administrator credentials and click LOGIN.Fig 1 — GANZ portal login pageStep 2 — Navigate to AI Rules > SharesAfter logging in, click Configuration in the top navigation bar. The left sidebar shows the full menu. The Video sources page (AI-Sources) is shown as a reference for the portal layout — the camera channels and their AI algorithms are listed here.Fig 2 — Configuration > AI Sources > Video sources (portal layout reference)In the left sidebar, expand AI-RULES and click Shares. The Shares page opens showing four category tabs: Network, Systemic, VMS, and NVR/DVR.Step 3 — Open the FTP ConfigurationOn the Shares page, ensure the Network tab is selected. Click the FTP sub-tab. The FTP configuration table is displayed. Initially the table is empty.Click the Add button (bottom right) to add a new FTP server entryFig 3 — AI Rules > Shares > Network > FTP tab with empty configuration and Add buttonStep 4 — Enter FTP CredentialsClicking Add opens the FTP configuration form. Fill in all fields as described in the table below. The FTP server credentials are obtained from the GCX Device Add API response.Action SettingsFieldValueNameA descriptive label for this FTP action (e.g. customer name or site)Time range of photos — From2 seconds before eventTime range of photos — To5 seconds after eventFTP Server SettingsFieldValueNameA label for the FTP server entryHostFTP server IP or hostname from GCX API ftpConfigPort21 (default FTP port)UsernameuniqueId value from GCX Device Add API responsePasswordPassword from GCX Device Add API responsePassive ModeUnchecked (enable only if behind NAT/strict firewall) The Host and Username values come from the ftpConfig object in the GCX Device Add API response (POST /api/v1/device). Use the uniqueId field as the FTP username.Fig 4 — FTP configuration form showing action name, time range, and FTP server credential fieldsStep 5 — Configure the File Path FormatBelow the FTP credentials, the File Path Format section lets you define the directory structure and filename for uploaded images using dynamic tokens.Click the File Path Format dropdown to select a base template (e.g. 'Basic file path template'), then click UseUse the Select to add tokens dropdown to insert additional tokens such as system time, MAC address, channel, or trigger nameThe preview at the bottom shows a sample path based on the selected tokensThe recommended default format generates a date-organised folder structure: }/ }/ }/ }/ }_ }_ }_ } } } } } }. }_ }.jpgThis produces a sample path such as:2019/07/01/18/00115F2A0024_3_My AI Trigger_20190701181020.216_1561961100.012345.jpgThe larger the photo resolution configured on the camera, the fewer photos per second can be sent via FTP. If image size is large, the number of photos in the configured time window may be reduced.Fig 5 — File Path Format builder with token selection, path preview, and Test buttonStep 6 — Test and ApplyClick the TEST button next to Send test file — this uploads a test image to the FTP serverIf the test succeeds (no error message), the credentials and path format are correctClick APPLY to save the entire FTP configurationEvents detected by the AI rules will now be automatically uploaded to the FTP serverAlways click TEST before APPLY. A successful test confirms that

title: GC Surge API Integration Guide for Sending Events to NxGen Platform
excerpt: GCXSurge API Integration Guide (Developer Documentation)IntroductionThis document explains how developers can send events to the NxGen platform using the GCXSurge Event API.Event API EndpointPOST https://gcxsurgeproxy.nxgen.cloud/api/v1/eventsAuthent
content: GCXSurge API Integration Guide (Developer Documentation)IntroductionThis document explains how developers can send events to the NxGen platform using the GCXSurge Event API.Event API EndpointPOST https://gcxsurgeproxy.nxgen.cloud/api/v1/eventsAuthenticationAuthentication is done using the API key provided during device creation.Add the following header:x-api-key: <token>Example:x-api-key: f56ce1453e2e76fd63077c1c9b7653f7e3b2ec0d19e716df6a3538be4afbe245.7a453b5e62692865058b0d64b67015e6.76c31c2f823e2af9ebd1c2afa35f09a5a556194fa16a8742Request FormatThe API accepts JSON payloads.Example request:POST /api/v1/eventsContent-Type: application/jsonx-api-key: <token>Body example: ], "clip": , "eventTime": "2026-03-13 16:41:44"} Please pass clip or images (Priority will be given to clip if both passed)In the image / clip type can be url or base64 Successful ResponseExample: , "event": , "_links": }Error ResponseExample: , "requestId": null, "timestamp": "2026-03-13T13:47:31.739Z"}

title: GC Surge Device Integration with NxGen Platform: API, Email, and FTP Methods
excerpt: GCXSurge IntegrationIntroductionThis document explains how GCXSurge devices integrate with the NxGen platform using the existing Add Device API.The integration supports multiple ingestion methods for events generated by GCXSurge devices.Supported int
content: GCXSurge IntegrationIntroductionThis document explains how GCXSurge devices integrate with the NxGen platform using the existing Add Device API.The integration supports multiple ingestion methods for events generated by GCXSurge devices.Supported integrations:API IntegrationEmail IntegrationFTP IntegrationThese integrations allow GCXSurge systems to send events, images, and metadata to the NxGen platform for processing and analytics.Enable GCXSurge Device on service providerAdd this property in service provider level Custom property.Parameter Name: GCXSurge_Device_Custom_baseUrlParameter Value: https://gcxsurgeproxy.nxgen.cloud/Device Creation FlowA GCXSurge device is created using the existing Add Device API with additional parameters specific to GCXSurge.Existing ParametersPlease have a loook on how existing Add Device API is working and prepare the payload.Use these fixed values for GCXSurge devices:catalogId: fc3d0c1df8d6477f81a8ca7205fce101manafacturer: GCXSurgeNew PropertygcxSurgeIntegrationsType:Array<string>Possible values:apiIntegrationemailIntegrationftpIntegrationExample payload: Note:The field is defined as an array.Currently only one value should be sent.Future versions may support multiple integrations simultaneously.Integration ResponseAfter device creation, the API returns integration configuration details depending on the selected integration type.These configurations allow GCXSurge systems to send events to the NxGen platform.Response structure: }}API IntegrationFor API integration, the response returns:API TokenEvent ingestion URLExample response: }}The GCXSurge system must send events to the eventsUrl using the provided token.Authentication is done using the header:x-api-key: <token>Email IntegrationFor Email integration, the response returns SMTP configuration.Example response: } }}FTP Integration For FTP integration, the response returns FTP credentials.Example response: } }}Events Validation and ProcessingThe code for receiving the events are written this microservice: https://gcxsurgeproxy.nxgen.cloud/Detailed diagramSync flow (teal): All three entry points (HTTP API, email link, FTP link) converge into Extract & Normalize, then pass through five middleware stages — device validation (Redis), token presence check, token validation (MongoDB), subscription/quota validation (MongoDB), and image processing. Any middleware failure exits right via the error path.Bridge (purple): On success, the pipeline is stashed in flow context and a 202 is sent immediately. A fresh asyncMsg (no req/res) is created to avoid ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT.Async flow (amber): Picks up the pipeline from context, increments the usage quota, stamps lastUsedAt, builds the ingest payload, and fires POST → EventIngestRefactor.Error paths (red): Sync errors return structured JSON via the HTTP response node. Async errors are caught and logged only — they never touch the HTTP connection.

title: GC Surge Pricing Model
excerpt: GC Surge Pricing Model DocumentationOverviewGC Surge uses a usage-based pricing model centered on cameras rather than alarm packages. Customers are billed per camera per month, with volume discounts applied automatically based on total camera count.T
content: GC Surge Pricing Model DocumentationOverviewGC Surge uses a usage-based pricing model centered on cameras rather than alarm packages. Customers are billed per camera per month, with volume discounts applied automatically based on total camera count.There is no package selection and no upfront payment requirement.Pricing StructureCamera-Based Pricing TiersPricing decreases as the number of cameras increases.Camera VolumePrice per CameraIncluded Alarms per CameraOverage Cost< 500€3.003,000€0.03/alarm< 2,500€2.803,000€0.03/alarm< 5,000€2.703,000€0.03/alarm< 10,000€2.603,000€0.03/alarm10,000+€2.503,000€0.03/alarmKey RulesEach camera includes 3,000 alarms per monthOverage is charged at a flat rate of €0.03 per alarmVolume discounts apply only to the per-camera price, not overageTrial ModelActivation RequirementsA valid credit card is required before the trial startsThe card is authorized for future automatic billingTrial EntitlementsDuration: 14 daysUp to 10 cameras300 alarms per camera includedAI processing (NOVA99x) is disabledOnly ZenMode is availableTrial BehaviorAlarm HandlingAlarms are ingested and visible in the UIEach camera has a limit of 300 alarms during trialThreshold BehaviorOnce a camera exceeds 300 alarms:False alarm filtering is disabledAlarms continue to be ingestedAI processing is not applied unless overage is acceptedOverage During TrialAny alarm beyond 300 per camera is charged at €0.03 per alarmCharges are added to the end-of-month invoiceUnlocking AI During TrialCustomer must explicitly accept overage chargesOnce accepted, NOVA99x processing becomes availableReset LogicAt the end of the trial:Alarm credits reset to 0System transitions into paid modelBilling LogicBillable CamerasA camera is billable only if it generates 5 or more alarms during the billing periodInvoice GenerationInvoices are generated automatically at the end of each monthInvoice ComponentsCamera charges (based on active cameras)Overage charges (alarms exceeding included limits)Key Differences from Previous ModelOld ModelCustomers purchased alarm packagesUpfront payment requiredFixed alarm bucketsNew ModelNo packagesNo upfront paymentBilling based on active camerasPay-as-you-go overage modelExample ScenariosExample 1Customer has 400 camerasPrice per camera: €3.00Total base cost: €1,200/monthIf each camera generates 3,500 alarms:Included: 3,000 per cameraOverage: 500 alarms per cameraOverage cost per camera: €15Total overage: €6,000Total monthly bill: €7,200Example 2Customer has 3,000 camerasPrice per camera: €2.70Only cameras with >=5 alarms are billedIf 2,500 cameras are active:Billable cameras: 2,500Base cost: €6,750

title: GC Surge — High-Performance Alarm Processing
excerpt:
GC Surge — Coming Soon GC Surge is an upcoming high-performance feature for GCXONE that dramatically increases alarm processing throughput during peak demand periods. This article will be updated with full documentation when the feature launches.
Wha
content:
GC Surge — Coming Soon GC Surge is an upcoming high-performance feature for GCXONE that dramatically increases alarm processing throughput during peak demand periods. This article will be updated with full documentation when the feature launches.
What to Expect
Intelligent load balancing across alarm processing nodes
Priority queuing for critical alarm types during surge conditions
Real-time throughput monitoring and automatic scaling
Configurable surge thresholds by tenant and alarm category
Full audit trail of surge events and processing decisions
Current Status GC Surge is currently in development. Planned release: Q2 2026. Contact your NXGEN account manager for early access information.

title: HikVision FTP
excerpt: FTP-Based Event TransmissionHikvision Camera Configuration GuideIntegration with GCX-Surge Analytics ServiceOverviewThis document describes how to configure FTP-based event transmission for Hikvision cameras so that events are automatically sent to G
content: FTP-Based Event TransmissionHikvision Camera Configuration GuideIntegration with GCX-Surge Analytics ServiceOverviewThis document describes how to configure FTP-based event transmission for Hikvision cameras so that events are automatically sent to GCX-Surge for analytics processing.The workflow involves two main steps:Registering the device using the GCX Device Add API to obtain FTP credentialsConfiguring the Hikvision camera to send event snapshots to the FTP serverStep 1 — Register Device via APIBefore configuring the camera, register the device in the GCX platform using the Device Add API. This will return the FTP credentials needed for camera configuration.API EndpointPOST https://qagcapi02.nxgen.cloud/api/v1/deviceRequest Body (form-data)imageFile — image file associated with the devicepayload — JSON array with device fields: id, status, armstate, sensorType, commentSuccessful ResponseA 200 OK response returns an apiToken object containing the FTP configuration. The key fields to note are:serviceType: "ftp" — confirms FTP integrationuniqueId — use this as the FTP username on the cameraftpConfig — contains the FTP server address and credentialsFig 1 — Postman showing successful device registration response (200 OK) with FTP credentialsStep 2 — Configure Hikvision CameraLog in to the Hikvision camera web portal (e.g., http://192.168.1.112) and follow the steps below.2.1 Local SettingsNavigate to Configuration > Local. Verify the live view and recording settings. Ensure Protocol is set to TCP and record file size is appropriate for your storage.Fig 2 — Configuration > Local: Live View and Record File Settings2.2 Configure FTP ServerNavigate to Configuration > Network > Advanced Settings > FTP tab and fill in the following fields:Server Address — IP or hostname returned in the API ftpConfigPort — default 21User Name — use the uniqueId value from the API responsePassword — as provided in the API responseDirectory Structure — Set to "Save in the root directory"Picture Filing Interval — OFF (events upload in real time)Picture Name — DefaultUpload Picture — check this checkboxClick Test to verify the credentials. If the test succeeds, click Save.Fig 3 — FTP configuration with server address, credentials, and Upload Picture enabled2.3 Enable Motion DetectionNavigate to Configuration > Event > Basic Event > Motion Detection.Check Enable Motion DetectionOptionally check Enable Dynamic Analysis for Motion to highlight moving objects in live viewUnder Area Settings, draw the detection area by clicking Draw Area and selecting the desired zone on the camera viewSet Sensitivity to an appropriate level (80 is recommended as a starting point)Fig 4 — Motion Detection enabled with detection area drawn and sensitivity set to 802.4 Configure Linkage MethodStill on the Motion Detection page, click the Linkage Method tab and configure the following:Under Normal Linkage: enable Notify Surveillance Center and Upload to FTP/Memory CardClick Save after configuring the linkage method.Fig 5 — Linkage Method: Upload to FTP/Memory Card and Trigger Recording enabled2.5 Configure Record ScheduleNavigate to Configuration > Storage > Schedule Settings > Record Schedule.Check Enable to activate recordingSelect Event from the type dropdown and draw time slots, or leave as Continuous to record all the timeClick SaveRecommendation: Enabling the record schedule is required on some Hikvision models before FTP event uploads begin working. Even if you only want event-triggered snapshots, ensure recording is enabled.Fig 6 — Record Schedule enabled for all days (purple = Event recording)2.6 Enable Event-Triggered SnapshotNavigate to Configuration > Storage > Schedule Settings > Capture > Capture Parameters tab.Under the Event-Triggered section:Check Enable Event-Triggered SnapshotFormat — JPEGResolution — 1280x720 (or higher if required)Quality — HighInterval — 1000 millisecondsCapture Number — 1 (number of snapshots per event)Click Save. The camera will now automatically upload snapshots to the FTP server whenever a motion detection event occurs.Fig 7 — Capture Parameters: Event-Triggered Snapshot enabled with JPEG/720p/High quality settings

title: Vivotek IP Camera FTP
excerpt: VIVOTEK IB9387-EHTV-V3 | IP CameraFTP-Based Event TransmissionEvent Server & Media Configuration GuideGCX-Surge Analytics IntegrationOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on a VIVOTEK IP camera. When a detection e
content: VIVOTEK IB9387-EHTV-V3 | IP CameraFTP-Based Event TransmissionEvent Server & Media Configuration GuideGCX-Surge Analytics IntegrationOverviewThis guide explains how to configure FTP-based event image uploads on a VIVOTEK IP camera. When a detection event occurs, the camera will automatically upload snapshots to the GCX-Surge FTP server for analytics processing.Configuration requires three areas within the VIVOTEK web portal:System > Network > Service — enable the FTP service on the cameraEvent > Event server & media — add the FTP server and define the snapshot mediaEvent > Event (Rules) — link the server and media to an existing event rulePrerequisitesFTP credentials from the GCX Device Add API response (host, port, username, password)Administrator access to the VIVOTEK camera web portalThe camera must have active internet accessAt least one event rule (e.g. gcxintrusion) already configured under EventConfiguration StepsFollow the steps below in order.1Open a browser and navigate to the VIVOTEK camera IP address. Log in with administrator credentials.2Navigate to System > Network > Service tab. Enable the FTP server toggle and click Save.3Navigate to Event in the left sidebar. Click the Event server & media tab.4Under Event server, click + Add. Set Server type to FTP and enter the GCX credentials.5Click Test to verify the FTP connection. If successful, click Save.6Under Event media, click + Add. Enter a media name, set Media type to Snapshot.7Configure pre/post-event buffer, custom image frequency, and enable Add date and time suffix to file name. Click Save.8Click the Event tab. Find your existing event rule (e.g. gcxintrusion) and click the edit (pencil) icon.9Navigate to the Action step. Under Event server & media, check surge ftp and select surge media from the Media dropdown.10Click Save. Events will now be automatically uploaded to the GCX FTP server.Step 1 — Log In to the VIVOTEK PortalOpen a web browser and enter the camera's IP address. The VIVOTEK login page displays the camera model (IB9387-EHTV-V3). Enter your administrator username and password and click Login.Fig 1 — VIVOTEK IB9387-EHTV-V3 login pageStep 2 — Enable FTP ServiceNavigate to System > Network in the left sidebar. Click the Service tab. Scroll down to the FTP section and enable the Enable FTP server toggle. Set FTP port to 21. Click Save.Enabling the FTP server on the camera is required before the camera can upload files to an external FTP server via event actions.Fig 2 — System > Network > Service: Enable FTP server toggle turned on, port 21Steps 3 – 5 — Add the FTP Event ServerNavigate to Event in the left sidebar. Click the Event server & media tab. The page shows two sections: Event server and Event media, both empty initially.Fig 3 — Event > Event server & media tab showing empty Event server and Event media sectionsUnder Event server, click + Add to open the Event server dialog. Fill in the following fields:FieldValueServer namesurge ftp (or any descriptive label)Server typeFTPServer address51.102.164.94 (from GCX API ftpConfig)Server port21User nameuniqueId value from GCX Device Add API responsePasswordPassword from GCX Device Add API responseFTP folder name / (root directory)Passive modeUncheckedClick Test to verify the FTP connection — a success message confirms the credentials are correctClick Save to save the event serverThe Server address and User name come from the ftpConfig object returned by the GCX Device Add API (POST /api/v1/device). Use the uniqueId field as the FTP username.Fig 4 — Event server dialog: surge ftp FTP server configured with host, port, username and root folder pathSteps 6 – 7 — Add the Event MediaUnder Event media, click + Add to open the Event media dialog. Configure the media as follows:FieldValueMedia namesurge media (or any descriptive label)Media typeSnapshotSourceStream 1Pre-event buffer (seconds)1Post-event buffer (seconds)1Custom image frequency (frames/s)1File name prefixLeave blank (optional)Add date and time suffix to file nameCheckedClick Save to save the event mediaThe maximum number of images per event is affected by the video source resolution and whether display timestamp and video title overlays are enabled. Higher resolution means fewer images can be sent per second.Fig 5 — Event media dialog: surge media configured as Snapshot from Stream 1 with date/time suffix enabledSteps 8 – 9 — Link Server & Media to an Event RuleClick the Event tab to see the list of configured event rules. Rules such as gcxintrusion and gcxloitering are displayed here. Click the edit (pencil) icon on the event rule you want to configure.Fig 6 — Event tab showing gcxintrusion and gcxloitering rules (trigger source: vadp, schedule: 00:00–24:00 all days)The event wizard opens. Navigate through the steps to reach the Action step (step 3). In the Event server & media section:Check the surge ftp chec
